title: What approaches has FDA accepted for non-proportional hazards in immuno-oncology survival analyses?
subtitle: Cite the reviewer language
description: An agent pulls FDA review documents that flag non-proportional hazards in immuno-oncology survival analyses, extracts the sensitivity-analysis methods FDA has accepted (or pushed back on), and quotes reviewer language directly.

events.content: What approaches has FDA accepted for non-proportional hazards in immuno-oncology survival analyses? Cite the reviewer language.

I'll look for FDA review documents where NPH was material — typically PD-(L)1 inhibitors and CAR-T programs with delayed separation — and extract what sensitivity analyses the reviewer asked for or accepted.

events.args: paperclip search "non-proportional hazards delayed separation restricted mean survival immuno-oncology FDA" -s fda -n 18

events.content:
Found 14 documents  [s_nph]

  Sources: fda(14)

  1. Statistical Review — BLA 125514/S-088 (pembro + chemo, NSCLC)
  2. Statistical Review — BLA 761049/S-024 (durva, PACIFIC update)
  3. Statistical Review — BLA 761034/S-031 (atezo, IMbrave150)
  4. Statistical Review — BLA 761346 (PD-1, advanced NSCLC)
  5. Multi-Disc Review — BLA 761317 (tislelizumab, ESCC)
  6. Statistical Review — BLA 125514/S-110 (pembro, neoadjuvant TNBC)
  7. Advisory Committee briefing — survival analysis in IO (2022)
  8. Statistical Review — BLA 125499/S-020 (nivolumab, esophageal)
  ... [6 more]

events.args:
paperclip filter --from s_nph \
  "reviewer specifically discusses non-proportional hazards methodology (RMST, weighted log-rank, milestone analysis, max-combo) — exclude documents only mentioning NPH in passing"

paperclip map --from s_nph_meth \
  "Extract: (1) trial, (2) NPH evidence cited by reviewer, (3) sensitivity analyses the sponsor proposed, (4) the reviewer's specific position on each (accepted / accepted with caveat / rejected), and (5) any direct reviewer quote."

events.content:
Map complete: 8/8 documents  [m_nph_meth]

  ✓ BLA 125514/S-088 (pembro + chemo, NSCLC)
    NPH evidence: visual delayed separation in OS K-M, Schoenfeld
    residual p < 0.01. Sponsor provided: HR with 95% CI as
    primary; RMST difference at 24mo as supportive.
    Reviewer: accepted HR as primary 'given the magnitude of
    effect, the HR interpretation is conservative under NPH';
    accepted RMST as supportive. 'Restricted mean survival
    time at the prespecified landmark provides a useful
    complementary effect measure when the proportional hazards
    assumption is not strictly met.'

  ✓ BLA 761049/S-024 (durva, PACIFIC update)
    NPH evidence: clear delayed separation, Schoenfeld p < 0.001
    on long-term f/u. Sponsor: piecewise HR + RMST at 60mo.
    Reviewer accepted RMST as primary supportive analysis;
    cautioned against using a single HR estimate as the sole
    summary. 'The hazard ratio estimate should be interpreted
    with caution given clear evidence of a delayed-separation
    pattern; we recommend restricted mean survival time as a
    sensitivity analysis.'

  ✓ BLA 761346 (PD-1, advanced NSCLC)
    NPH evidence: visual but Schoenfeld marginal. Sponsor:
    weighted log-rank G(0,1) as sensitivity. Reviewer: 'the
    weighted log-rank with late-emphasis weighting is
    inappropriate when used as a primary efficacy test
    rather than as a sensitivity analysis; we accept it as
    sensitivity only.'

  ✓ BLA 761317 (tislelizumab, ESCC)
    NPH evidence: visual late-separation. Sponsor: max-combo
    test as primary. Reviewer pushed back: 'a max-combo
    test should not replace the prespecified log-rank as
    the primary inferential basis; we accept it only as
    supportive evidence and request the prespecified test
    remain the basis for labeling.'

  ✓ BLA 125499/S-020 (nivolumab, esophageal)
    NPH evidence: minimal. Sponsor: HR primary, RMST sensitivity.
    Reviewer accepted both without commentary.

  ✓ AC briefing — survival analysis in IO (2022)
    Agency position: RMST is the preferred secondary analysis
    when NPH is plausible. Max-combo and weighted log-rank
    are acceptable as sensitivity only; not as primary tests.
    Milestone analyses (e.g., 24-month survival rate) are
    acceptable supportive analyses.

  ... [3 more aligned with the same picture]

## FDA accepted approaches for non-proportional hazards in IO survival

The agency's position has stabilized into a clear hierarchy: **HR with the prespecified log-rank remains the inferential primary**, with **RMST as the preferred secondary** when NPH is plausible, and **weighted log-rank / max-combo only as sensitivity**, not as the basis for labeling.

### What the recent record shows

| Analysis | Status with FDA | Reviewer language |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **HR + prespecified log-rank** | Primary inferential basis, retained even under modest NPH | "the prespecified test [should] remain the basis for labeling" |
| **Restricted mean survival time (RMST)** at a prespecified landmark | Preferred supportive analysis under NPH | "RMST at the prespecified landmark provides a useful complementary effect measure when PH is not strictly met" |
| **Piecewise hazard ratio** | Acceptable supportive | Used in PACIFIC follow-up with reviewer acceptance |
| **Milestone survival rate** (e.g., 24mo OS) | Acceptable supportive | 2022 AC briefing endorsed |
| **Weighted log-rank (G-class)** | Sensitivity only; **not primary** | "inappropriate when used as a primary efficacy test rather than as a sensitivity analysis" |
| **Max-combo test** | Sensitivity only; **not primary** | "a max-combo test should not replace the prespecified log-rank as the primary inferential basis" |

### Three concrete reviewer quotes worth memorizing

> *Restricted mean survival time at the prespecified landmark provides a useful complementary effect measure when the proportional hazards assumption is not strictly met.*
>
> — Statistical Review, BLA 125514/S-088 (pembrolizumab + chemo, NSCLC)

> *The hazard ratio estimate should be interpreted with caution given clear evidence of a delayed-separation pattern; we recommend restricted mean survival time as a sensitivity analysis.*
>
> — Statistical Review, BLA 761049/S-024 (durvalumab, PACIFIC update)

> *A max-combo test should not replace the prespecified log-rank as the primary inferential basis; we accept it only as supportive evidence and request the prespecified test remain the basis for labeling.*
>
> — Multi-Disciplinary Review, BLA 761317 (tislelizumab, ESCC)

### Pre-specification recommendations

For a new IO trial likely to have NPH:

1. **Keep HR + log-rank as the primary inferential test** even though it is conservative under NPH — the alternative (weighted log-rank or max-combo as primary) will not be accepted as the labeling basis.
2. **Pre-specify RMST at a single landmark** (24 or 36 months are most common) as the principal supportive analysis.
3. **Pre-specify milestone survival rates** at clinically meaningful landmarks (12, 24, 36 mo) — these are uncontroversial and reinforce the RMST result.
4. **Reserve max-combo or weighted log-rank for sensitivity** with explicit positioning in the SAP. Do not pre-specify them as primary alternatives — the agency will push back at review time.

### Why this matters

The IO field has consistently produced curves that violate PH at long follow-up. Sponsors that pre-specify NPH-aware analyses upfront — RMST and milestones, not max-combo as primary — avoid a recurring conversation that adds time and friction to the review cycle.

*Documents read: 12. Wall clock: 199 seconds.*
